name: Chlorine
name_origin: From the Greek 'chloros' meaning pale green or yellow-green, referring to the color of the gas.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Chlorine is the 21st most abundant element in Earth's crust (~145 ppm) and the most abundant halogen in seawater. Its high reactivity means it does not occur as the free element in nature; it is found mainly as the chloride ion in salts.

compounds.name: Hydrogen chloride / Hydrochloric acid
compounds.summary: A strong mineral acid widely used in metal pickling, food processing, and chemical synthesis; produced naturally in the stomach as gastric acid.

compounds.name: Sodium hypochlorite
compounds.summary: The active ingredient in liquid bleach and a common disinfectant for water and surfaces.

compounds.name: Polyvinyl chloride
compounds.summary: A widely used thermoplastic polymer for pipes, flooring, cable insulation, and medical tubing.

compounds.name: Chloroform
compounds.summary: An organic solvent and historic anesthetic, now mainly used as a precursor to PTFE refrigerants.

compounds.name: Potassium chloride
compounds.summary: A potassium fertilizer (potash), salt substitute, and component of lethal-injection mixtures.

compounds.name: Chlorine dioxide
compounds.summary: A powerful disinfectant and bleach used in drinking water treatment and pulp processing.

uses: Disinfection of drinking water and swimming pools
Production of polyvinyl chloride (PVC) and other chlorinated plastics
Bleaching agent in the pulp, paper, and textile industries
Manufacture of solvents, pharmaceuticals, and pesticides
Production of hydrochloric acid for metal cleaning and processing
Active ingredient in many household cleaners and bleach products
reactivity_summary: Chlorine is a highly reactive yellow-green diatomic gas with a sharp, choking odor; it is a strong oxidizing agent and the second most reactive halogen after fluorine. It reacts directly with nearly all elements (except noble gases and a few unreactive metals) and forms acidic solutions in water.
isotopes_summary: Chlorine has two stable isotopes, Cl-35 (~75.8%) and Cl-37 (~24.2%). The most notable radioisotope is Cl-36, with a half-life of about 301,000 years, used in geological and groundwater dating.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Chlorine
